%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 5.5-1 The Periodic Nature of Frequency Response

> **Section Objective:** Learn why discrete-time frequency response repeats every \(2\pi\), and how to reduce any discrete-time sinusoid to its apparent frequency.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Periodic frequency response
- Non-unique discrete-time sinusoids
- Fundamental band
- Apparent frequency
- Phase reversal

## 1. Frequency Response Repeats Every \(2\pi\)

This formula states that the frequency response \(H(e^{j\Omega})\) of any discrete-time LTI system is **periodic in \(\Omega\) with period \(2\pi\)**.

**Symbol guide:**
- \(H(e^{j\Omega})\) — the frequency response of the system, evaluated at discrete-time frequency \(\Omega\)
- \(\Omega\) — discrete-time frequency in radians per sample
- \(m\) — any integer (positive, negative, or zero)
- \(\mathbb{Z}\) — the set of all integers

**When to use it:** Any time a problem asks whether two frequency-response values are equal, or asks you to plot \(H(e^{j\Omega})\) outside a single \(2\pi\)-wide interval.

### EXAM TRIGGER

If you see \(\Omega + 2\pi m\) in a frequency-response expression, the periodicity formula applies immediately.

### COMMON MISUSE

Do not treat \(H(e^{j\Omega})\) like a continuous-time frequency response that produces new behavior for every new value of \(\Omega\). In discrete time, the response is the same at \(\Omega\), \(\Omega + 2\pi\), \(\Omega - 2\pi\), and so on.

$$H\!\left(e^{j(\Omega+2\pi m)}\right)=H\!\left(e^{j\Omega}\right),\quad m\in\mathbb{Z}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 2. Discrete-Time Sinusoids Are Not Unique

Adding any integer multiple of \(2\pi\) to a discrete-time frequency produces **exactly the same sample sequence**.

**Why it works:** For any integer sample index \(n\) and any integer \(m\):

$$e^{j2\pi mn} = \left(e^{j2\pi}\right)^{mn} = 1^{mn} = 1$$

so \(e^{j(\Omega+2\pi m)n} = e^{j\Omega n} \cdot e^{j2\pi mn} = e^{j\Omega n}\). The cosine version follows by taking the real part.

**Minimal example:**

$$e^{j(0.3\pi + 2\pi)n} = e^{j2.3\pi n} = e^{j0.3\pi n}$$

Frequencies \(0.3\pi\) and \(2.3\pi\) produce identical discrete-time samples.

### EXAM TRIGGER

If two frequencies differ by \(2\pi\), \(4\pi\), \(6\pi\), or any \(2\pi m\), the sequences are identical.

### COMMON MISUSE

Do not judge frequency by the written number alone. Always check equivalence modulo \(2\pi\) before concluding two sinusoids are different.

$$e^{j(\Omega+2\pi m)n}=e^{j\Omega n},\quad m\in\mathbb{Z}$$

## 3. Fold Any Frequency Into the Fundamental Band

**Eq. (5.38)** defines \(\Omega_a\), the **principal** (or modulo-\(2\pi\)) version of \(\Omega\), chosen to lie inside the fundamental band \(-\pi \le \Omega_a < \pi\).

**Symbol guide:**
- \(\Omega\) — the original discrete-time frequency (any real value)
- \(m\) — the integer chosen so that \(\Omega_a\) lands in \([-\pi, \pi)\)
- \(\Omega_a\) — the equivalent frequency inside the fundamental band

**Two worked examples:**

$$\Omega = 1.6\pi: \quad 1.6\pi = -0.4\pi + 2\pi \implies \Omega_a = -0.4\pi$$

$$\Omega = 2.3\pi: \quad 2.3\pi = 0.3\pi + 2\pi \implies \Omega_a = 0.3\pi$$

**When to use it:** Any time an exam asks for the equivalent discrete-time frequency, the fundamental-band frequency, or the apparent frequency — apply Eq. (5.38) first.

### COMMON MISUSE

Some problems use the band \([0, 2\pi)\) instead of \([-\pi, \pi)\). If the problem specifically asks for the fundamental band, use \([-\pi, \pi)\) as defined by Eq. (5.38). Do not substitute \(0 \le \Omega_a < 2\pi\) unless the problem explicitly allows it.

$$\Omega_a=\Omega-2\pi m,\quad -\pi\le\Omega_a<\pi,\quad m\in\mathbb{Z}$$

## 4. Apparent Frequency and Phase Reversal for Real Sinusoids

For a real cosine sinusoid, the **apparent frequency** is the nonnegative value \(|\Omega_a|\), which always lies in \([0, \pi]\).

**Critical rule:** If \(\Omega_a < 0\), you must rewrite the cosine using the positive frequency **and reverse the sign of the phase**:

$$\cos(\Omega_a n + \theta) = \cos(-|\Omega_a| n + \theta) = \cos(|\Omega_a| n - \theta)$$

**Worked example:** Find the apparent form of \(\cos(1.6\pi n + \theta)\).

1. Apply Eq. (5.38): \(1.6\pi = -0.4\pi + 2\pi\), so \(\Omega_a = -0.4\pi\).
2. Apparent frequency: \(\Omega_{\mathrm{app}} = |-0.4\pi| = 0.4\pi\).
3. Since \(\Omega_a < 0\), phase sign reverses: apparent form is \(\cos(0.4\pi n - \theta)\).

### EXAM TRIGGER

Whenever the reduced \(\Omega_a\) is negative, the phase sign reversal step is mandatory.

### COMMON MISUSE

Taking \(|\Omega_a|\) for the frequency but forgetting to flip the sign of \(\theta\). Both steps must be applied together.

$$\Omega_{\mathrm{app}}=|\Omega_a|$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="trap_exposure" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Frequency response is periodic: \(H(e^{j(\Omega+2\pi m)})=H(e^{j\Omega})\) for all integers \(m\).
- Discrete-time sinusoids are non-unique: \(e^{j(\Omega+2\pi m)n}=e^{j\Omega n}\) exactly for integer \(n\).
- Eq. (5.38) folds any frequency to the fundamental band: \(\Omega_a=\Omega-2\pi m,\ -\pi\le\Omega_a<\pi\).
- Apparent frequency for real sinusoids: \(\Omega_{\mathrm{app}}=|\Omega_a|\); if \(\Omega_a<0\), phase sign reverses.
- In cycles/sample: \(F=\Omega/(2\pi)\) with fundamental range \([-0.5,\,0.5)\) cycles/sample.

*Next, we will use this periodicity to understand aliasing and sampling rate.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
